resumen: A night-shift employee learns why nobody at an old Kansas Walmart goes out the back door after two.
contenido: On the outskirts of Kansas, there are towns that never make it into movies. Flat, yellow, endless places where the wind moves through the cornfields like something enormous is breathing under the earth, and where the brightest building for twenty miles is usually a Walmart open late, its blue sign glowing over the empty parking lot like a cheap promise of safety.

I worked the night shift cleaning floors, bathrooms, and aisles after certain sections closed, even though technically the store stayed open twenty-four hours because there was always someone buying milk, ammo, diapers, or beer at three in the morning. It wasn’t a bad job if you didn’t mind the smell of bleach, the fluorescent lights, the strange customers, and that soft music playing over the speakers like they were trying to lull the dead to sleep.

The Walmart sat at the edge of town, right where the houses ended and the fields began, with a ditch behind it, a service road, and a row of dumpsters where employees smoked when the manager wasn’t looking. Behind those dumpsters was the cornfield, thick, dark, too close to the building, and for some reason nobody on the night shift went out the back door after two.
I asked about it my first week, and a cashier named Denise told me, without looking up from counting bills:
“Don’t feed the baby.”
I thought it was some local joke, one of those things older employees say to scare the new guy, so I laughed.
Denise didn’t.
“If you hear a baby crying behind the store, don’t go outside. If you see a crib, don’t touch it. If someone knocks on the loading door and sounds like a child, call the manager and step away.”
“What kind of town is this?” I asked.
She looked at me with tired eyes.
“The kind that’s still here because it learned the rules.”
I didn’t ask again.
Not until inventory night.

It was mid-August, that humid Midwest heat that gets under your clothes even in the middle of the night. The parking lot was almost empty, the ice machine by the entrance buzzed like a sick insect, and inside it smelled like detergent, old fried chicken, and wet cardboard. There were five of us in the store: Denise at the register, Rob in electronics, Marty the manager locked in the office going over reports, a new kid stocking toys, and me running the floor machine down the pet aisle.
At 2:13 a.m., the music stopped playing over the speakers.
It didn’t cut off suddenly. It drowned, like someone had pushed the song underwater.
Then we heard crying.
A baby.

Very faint at first, just a tiny whimper lost somewhere between the aisles, but loud enough for all of us to lift our heads. The sound was coming from the speakers, or at least that’s what I thought at first, except the voice had an echo to it, like it wasn’t recorded, like something was crying inside the walls.
Denise appeared at the end of the aisle, pale.
“Turn off the machine,” she said.
I did.
The crying kept going.
Rob came out of electronics with a flashlight in his hand and tried to joke, but his voice cracked halfway through.
“Maybe someone left a kid in the store.”
Denise shook her head.
“There’s no baby.”
Then someone knocked on the loading door.
Three soft knocks.
Knock, Knock, Knock.
The crying stopped immediately.
I will never forget that silence. It wasn’t relief. It was attention. Like when a predator stops moving because it just heard you breathe.
Marty came out of the office with his keys hanging from his belt and a pistol in his hand, because in Kansas even Walmart managers look prepared for a small apocalypse.
“Everybody to the front,” he ordered.
But the new kid, Caleb, wasn’t with us.

We found him in the baby aisle, standing in front of the folding cribs, staring toward the employee door that led to the loading area. His face had gone slack, like he was listening to a song only he understood.
“My little sister cries like that,” he murmured.
Denise grabbed his arm.
“You don’t have a little sister, Caleb.”
He looked at her without seeing her.
“I do now.”
The knock came again.
Knock, Knock, Knock.
And this time, a voice spoke from the other side of the door. Small, wet, trembling.
“Mommy?”
Caleb pulled away.

Marty yelled at him not to move. Denise blocked his path. Rob dropped the flashlight. But the kid ran anyway. He ran like someone had yanked a string tied to his chest. He reached the loading door and placed his hand on the metal bar.
I saw something through the small rectangular window.
Outside, under the yellow security light, there was a crib.
A white, old-fashioned wooden crib, sitting between the dumpsters and the black edge of the cornfield.
It was rocking by itself.
Caleb opened the door.

The night air came in like animal breath, thick, sweet, rotten. At the same time, the crying started again, but now it wasn’t coming from the speakers. It was coming from outside, from the crib, from the darkness behind the store.
“Caleb, get back!” Marty shouted.
The kid walked down the loading ramp.
The crib stopped rocking.

From where we stood, we couldn’t see inside it, only the white railing and a yellow blanket hanging over one side. Caleb approached slowly, hands raised, like he was about to pick up a sleeping baby. Denise started praying under her breath. Rob was crying without making a sound.
Marty aimed the gun, but he didn’t fire.
Caleb reached the crib.
He leaned over.
And something inside grabbed him.

It wasn’t a hand. A hand has shape, fingers, human intention. What came out of that crib was a thin, pale limb, too long, covered in wrinkled skin like a newborn’s, but stretched over bones that did not belong to a baby. It coiled around Caleb’s neck and yanked him down so hard his face slammed into the wood.
He screamed once.
Then the crib closed over him.

It shouldn’t have been able to. It didn’t have a lid. It was an open crib. But the rails folded inward like ribs, and the yellow blanket swelled, tightened, sank. We heard bones breaking inside. Not loudly, not like in a movie, but in small, wet, quick cracks, methodical, like someone snapping green branches.
Marty fired.
Once. Twice. Three times.
The crib went still.
The cornfield behind it began to move.
Not from the wind.
First one line opened between the stalks. Then another. Then many more, as if several large bodies were moving through the field, crouched low, dragging themselves toward the light.
Denise shoved me backward and screamed:
“Close the door!”
Marty didn’t move. He was still aiming.
The crib opened.
Caleb stepped out.
Or whatever was wearing Caleb did.

His body was whole, at least from a distance, but the shape was wrong. His shoulders were too high, his head tilted too far back, his mouth open in a loose, wet smile. His Walmart shirt was stained with something dark that looked like mud, and his stomach moved under the fabric like something alive was pushing from the inside.
“I’m fine,” it said in Caleb’s voice.
But its mouth didn’t move in time with the words.
Marty fired again.
Caleb’s head snapped to the side.
Then it slowly turned back to center.
The smile grew wider.
“Don’t feed the baby,” it said, mimicking Denise’s voice.
And then its stomach opened.

It didn’t explode. It didn’t tear open with blood the way you’d imagine. It opened like a sick flower, petals of skin peeling away from the belly button, and inside there were no organs. There was a small, wrinkled, white face with black eyes and a round mouth, a baby’s mouth full of old man teeth.
The thing inside Caleb cried.
That cry tore through the store.
The lights flickered.
From deep in the aisles, more cries began to answer. One from toys. One from pharmacy. Another near clothing. As if the whole store had suddenly filled with hidden babies.
Marty finally backed away.
Denise slammed the loading door shut and dropped the latch, but something hit the other side with Caleb’s body.
Once.
Twice.
Three times.
Each hit came with his voice.
“Let me in.”
Bang.
“I’m cold.”
Bang.
“Mommy.”
Denise looked at me, and for the first time I saw that she wasn’t scared like someone facing something new. She was scared like someone who knew exactly what came next.
“To the front,” she said. “Now.”

We ran down the main aisle while the fluorescent lights sparked above us. The security cameras moved on their own, tracking us. On the electronics monitors, there were images of cribs. Dozens of them placed throughout the aisles, in the cereal section, in garden, in front of self-checkout, all rocking slowly.
Rob tripped near the toys.
A baby doll fell from a shelf and started crying.
He stepped on it to push it away.
The doll bit his ankle.

It didn’t have teeth before. I’m sure of it. It was one of those cheap plastic dolls, but when Rob went down screaming, its mouth opened all the way to its ears, and a thin tongue like a worm slid out from inside. Denise tried to help him, but Rob was already being dragged under the shelf. Not by the doll, but by many small hands reaching out from between the toy boxes. Pale, wet hands with black nails.
The last thing he said was:
“Tell my mom—”
Then something folded him into an angle no body is meant to make.
We kept running.
At the entrance, the automatic doors wouldn’t open.
Outside, the parking lot was full of cribs.
Cribs lined up between the cars, in the handicap spots, beside the shopping carts, under the blue Walmart sign. All white. All rocking. All crying in different voices.
Some cried like babies.
Others cried like adults pretending to be babies.
Marty raised his gun toward the glass, but Denise pushed his arm down.
“Don’t break anything,” she whispered. “If you open a way out, they all get in.”
“Then what the hell do we do?” I asked.
Denise looked toward the garden section.
“The old side door.”

We crossed the store as the speakers came back on, but it wasn’t music anymore. It was a nursery rhyme, slow and distorted, a lullaby my grandmother used to sing in Spanish when I was little, even though I had never told anyone in that town about it.
Duérmete, mi niño…
I heard it and almost stopped.
My grandmother had been dead for years.
Denise slapped me so hard across the face that it brought me back.
“Don’t listen,” she said. “It gives you what you miss.”

We reached garden. The smell of damp soil was unbearable. Between bags of mulch and fertilizer, there was a metal door with an emergency exit sign. Marty pulled out his keys with trembling hands. He tried one, then another. From the aisles came the sound of something crawling fast, many limbs against the waxed floor, like wet dogs running with human fingernails.
The key went in.
The door opened a crack.
On the other side was the cornfield.
Not the side parking lot. Not the outside wall. Not the street.
The cornfield.
The stalks came right up to the door, packed tight, tall, dark, their leaves scraping the frame like fingers.
Marty whispered:
“That’s not possible.”
And then something behind us answered in Caleb’s voice:
“We learned the doors.”
Marty shoved Denise and me into the cornfield.
“Run!”
The thing dropped on him from the ceiling.

I didn’t see where it came from. I only saw a long white body, like a baby stretched to the size of a man, with skinny arms, legs bent backward, and a huge head with no eyes, only a circular mouth. It landed on Marty, wrapped its fingers around his face, and started sucking.
That sound still wakes me up.
It wasn’t biting. It was suction. Like it was pulling out not his blood, but his voice, his memory, everything that made him a person.
Denise dragged me into the cornfield.

We ran blind between the stalks, leaves cutting our faces and arms. Behind us, the Walmart was still lit up, but it looked farther away with every second, as if the field was stretching. The crying came from everywhere. Babies on the left. Babies on the right. Babies under the ground.
Denise told me not to look at the cribs if they appeared.
They appeared.

One after another, between the rows of corn, lit by a light that came from nowhere. Some had pink blankets. Others blue. Others yellow. In one, I saw a tiny hand gripping the bars. In another, I heard my mother’s voice. In another, my father’s, even though my father had never once called me anything tender while he was alive.
“Son,” a voice said from inside a crib. “Come here.”
Denise kept pulling me forward.
Then she stepped on something soft.
She looked down.
It was a small Spider-Man backpack, stained with mud.
Denise stopped.
I knew in that instant that I had lost her.
“My son had one just like that,” she whispered.
“Denise, no.”
The corn in front of us opened, and another crib appeared. This one was different from the rest. Older. Made of dark wood. A mobile hung over it, little stars and moons turning without wind.
From inside, a child’s voice said:
“Mommy, I waited.”
Denise let go of my hand.
I grabbed her arm, but she looked at me with a sadness so deep I understood that the thing didn’t just imitate voices. It imitated debts. It imitated what you never got to say goodbye to.
“Run,” she told me.
Then she walked toward the crib.
I didn’t scream. Not because I didn’t want to, but because something in the field opened many mouths at once and screamed for me.
I ran until my legs stopped being legs and became pain. I ran until the corn ended and I collapsed into a ditch beside the main road, under the palest sunrise I have ever seen.
A state trooper found me two hours later.

I told him there had been a Walmart, employees, a store full of cribs, a cornfield behind it, something that took people. He looked at me the way cops look at you when they think you’re high, but he called it in anyway.
They never found the Walmart.

Where it should have been, there was only an empty lot, a cracked concrete slab, tall grass, and an old sign lying face down. The whole town swore the store had closed eleven years earlier after a fire during the night shift. Five employees died, they told me. Or at least that’s what they believed, because they never found all the bodies.
I insisted I had worked there.
There were no records.
No contract.
No security footage.
No payroll.
Only my uniform in an evidence bag, a blue shirt with the Walmart logo and a dark stain on the chest that the lab couldn’t identify.
I moved out of Kansas two weeks later.
Now I live in Arizona, far from the fields, far from the corn, far from any Walmart open at night. I work during the day, sleep with white noise, and never go into a big-box store after ten.
But three nights ago, I got an email notification.
“Your job application has been accepted.”
I haven’t applied for any job.
The sender was an automated HR address.
The location said:
Walmart Supercenter — Old County Road, Kansas.
The message was short.
“Night shift. Enter through the back door. Don’t feed the baby.”
